wine_history.name: Roman vines along the Vosges foothills
wine_history.description: Roman colonists planted vines along the Vosges foothills from the first century AD, exploiting the warm dry corridor between the mountains and the Rhine. Pottery and amphora finds across the Bas-Rhin trace the earliest organised viticulture in the area that became Alsace.
wine_history.evidence: Wikipedia's Alsace wine reference and the CIVA producers' board both cite the Gallo-Roman origins of vine cultivation along the Vosges piedmont.

wine_history.name: Carolingian monastery vineyards, 9th to 14th century
wine_history.description: From the Carolingian era onward the abbeys of Murbach, Andlau, Marmoutier and Wissembourg held extensive vineyard donations across Alsace. The monks cleared terraces on the Vosges slopes and codified plot-by-plot quality long before secular owners did.
wine_history.evidence: The Murbach Abbey and Andlau Abbey Wikipedia entries record the monastic vineyard holdings that shaped early Alsatian viticulture, and the Alsace wine reference summarises the medieval church role.

wine_history.name: Hospices de Strasbourg historic cellar founded, 1395
wine_history.description: The hospital of Strasbourg began ageing wine in its cellar from 1395 to fund its charitable work, a tradition that continued for more than 600 years. The cellar still holds the celebrated 1472 cask, a famous historic Alsatian wine.
wine_history.evidence: The SICA Chais des Hospices de Strasbourg site documents the 600-year cellar tradition and the famous 1472 cuvee.

wine_history.name: Thirty Years' War devastates the vineyard, 1618 to 1648
wine_history.description: The Thirty Years' War left Alsace ravaged. Pillaged villages and abandoned vineyards shrank Alsatian wine production by an estimated three quarters before the Treaty of Munster in 1648 ceded most of the region to France and recovery began.
wine_history.evidence: The Thirty Years' War and Treaty of Munster Wikipedia entries together establish the devastation of Alsace and the 1648 cession to France.

wine_history.name: Phylloxera and the Reichsland years, 1871 to 1918
wine_history.description: After the Franco-Prussian war Alsace became the German Reichsland Elsass-Lothringen from 1871 to 1918. Phylloxera struck in the 1890s and the German administration pushed bulk production over quality, eroding the reputation Alsatian wine had built before annexation.
wine_history.evidence: The Reichsland Elsass-Lothringen reference and the Phylloxera Wikipedia entry both document the political and viticultural collapse of late-19th-century Alsace.

wine_history.name: Return to France and the quality rebuild, 1918 to 1939
wine_history.description: Restored to France in 1918, Alsace's growers replanted with noble varieties and began the slow rebuild from bulk production toward varietal quality. The CIVA's predecessor bodies emerged in this period to defend the region's commercial reputation.
wine_history.evidence: The Alsace wine reference and the Alsace-Lorraine Wikipedia entry both record the interwar shift back to noble varietal viticulture.

wine_history.name: Forced German annexation and vineyard damage, 1940 to 1945
wine_history.description: Nazi Germany annexed Alsace from 1940 to 1945, and the Colmar Pocket fighting of winter 1944-45 damaged vineyards from Kaysersberg to Sigolsheim. Recovery after liberation drove the push for AOC protection that defined the post-war decades.
wine_history.evidence: The Alsace-Lorraine Wikipedia entry and the Alsace wine reference both record the wartime annexation and the post-liberation reconstruction of the vineyard.

wine_history.name: Alsace AOC created, 1962
wine_history.description: The Alsace AOC was created in 1962, late in the French appellation roll-out. The 1962 decree set the framework for the varietal labelling system unique to France, where Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris and the other noble grapes appear on the bottle.
wine_history.evidence: The Alsace wine reference and the INAO official site both date the 1962 creation of the Alsace AOC.

wine_history.name: First Alsace Grand Cru AOC at Schlossberg, 1975
wine_history.description: The 1975 decree elevated Kientzheim's Schlossberg to the first Alsace Grand Cru AOC, giving the region its first single-vineyard classification. Schlossberg remained the only Grand Cru for eight years before the wider 1983 expansion.
wine_history.evidence: The Alsace Grand Cru Wikipedia reference and CIVA's grands-crus page both record the 1975 elevation of Schlossberg as the first Alsace Grand Cru AOC.

wine_history.name: Cremant d'Alsace AOC created, 1976
wine_history.description: The Cremant d'Alsace AOC was created in 1976 for traditional-method sparkling wine, mainly from Pinot Blanc, Pinot Gris, Riesling, Pinot Noir, Auxerrois and Chardonnay. It is today France's biggest-selling Cremant, ahead of Bourgogne and Loire.
wine_history.evidence: The Cremant d'Alsace Wikipedia reference and CIVA's official site both date the 1976 creation of the AOC and describe its grape composition.

wine_history.name: Grand Cru expansion to 51 climats, 1983 to 2007
wine_history.description: Across two decrees in 1983 and 1992, then revisions through 2007, the Alsace Grand Cru system expanded from one to 51 named climats. Each Grand Cru carries its own permitted varieties and yield rules, an unusual single-vineyard AOC structure.
wine_history.evidence: The Alsace Grand Cru Wikipedia reference and CIVA's grands-crus page together document the 1983 through 2007 expansion to 51 climats.

wine_history.name: Vendanges Tardives and Selection de Grains Nobles formalised, 2001
wine_history.description: A 2001 decree codified the Vendanges Tardives and Selection de Grains Nobles late-harvest categories for the four noble Alsace varieties, fixing minimum sugar levels and harvest dates. The framework gave Alsace its formal sweet-wine top tier.
wine_history.evidence: The Vendanges Tardives and Selection de Grains Nobles Wikipedia references both document the 2001 codification of the late-harvest categories.

wine_history.name: Cremant d'Alsace specifications tightened, 2011
wine_history.description: A revised 2011 cahier des charges tightened the Cremant d'Alsace rules on yields, pressing and minimum lees ageing, raising the floor of quality. The change pushed several producers toward longer-aged Brut Nature and vintage cuvees.
wine_history.evidence: The Cremant d'Alsace Wikipedia reference and CIVA both document the revised 2011 production rules that lifted the floor of the appellation.

wine_history.name: The biodynamic boom, 1990s to 2010s
wine_history.description: Alsace became a major French biodynamic region. Marcel Deiss, Zind-Humbrecht, Ostertag, Pierre Frick, Domaine Weinbach and others converted across the 1990s and 2000s, and the region carries one of the highest Demeter and Biodyvin certifications per hectare in France.
wine_history.evidence: Demeter France and Biodyvin both list a heavy Alsatian membership across the Cote d'Or peers, and the Decanter Alsace guide notes the regional adoption rate.

wine_history.name: Pinot Noir admitted to Grand Cru, 2022
wine_history.description: A 2022 decree admitted Pinot Noir to three Alsace Grands Crus, Hengst, Kirchberg de Barr and Vorbourg, the first reds in the appellation. The change crowned three decades of warmer vintages and serious Pinot Noir work from Marcel Deiss, Albert Mann and others.
wine_history.evidence: The Hengst Wikipedia reference and Decanter's Alsace coverage both document the 2022 admission of Pinot Noir to three Grands Crus.

wine_history.name: Climate change and earlier harvests, 1990 to 2025
wine_history.description: Harvest dates across Alsace have moved roughly two to three weeks earlier since 1990 as average growing-season temperatures have risen. Picking that once started in late September now routinely begins in late August, reshaping varietal choice and Grand Cru rule revisions.
wine_history.evidence: The CIVA's terroir pages and the Alsace wine reference both note the harvest-shift trend tied to warmer vintages since 1990.
